> **Goal:** understand what machine learning actually *is* — learning a function
> from data — and the handful of ideas (model, parameters, loss, train/test,
> generalization) that every algorithm in the field rests on.

## Why a mathematician should care

A machine-learning model is nothing more than a **function with parameters**. We
do not write its rules by hand; we *learn* the parameters from examples by
minimizing a loss. You already have the tools — vectors and matrices to hold the
data, derivatives and gradients to do the minimizing. This chapter assembles them
into the conceptual frame that the rest of the course fills in.

## What is in the notebook

`09_intro_ml.ipynb` is mostly conceptual, with small runnable demos in pure
NumPy + Matplotlib. Run each code cell with **Shift + Enter**.

## Core ideas in this chapter

### What machine learning is
- ML = **learning a function from data**. We approximate an unknown true function
  $g$, where $y = g(x)$, by a learned function $f$ built from examples.
- **Supervised** learning uses labelled pairs $(x, y)$; **unsupervised** learning
  uses inputs only. Supervised splits into **regression** (numeric $y$) and
  **classification** (categorical $y$).

### The data layout
- The **feature matrix** $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$: rows = samples,
  columns = features.
- The **label vector** $y \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$: one answer per row.
- Mantra: **rows are samples, columns are features.**

### Model and parameters
- A **model** is a function $f_\theta$ with **parameters** $\theta$.
- Simplest example, linear regression: $f_\theta(x) = w x + b$ with
  $\theta = (w, b)$; or $f_\theta(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}\cdot\mathbf{x} + b$.

### Loss functions
- **Regression — Mean Squared Error:**
  $\mathrm{MSE}(\theta) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_i (f_\theta(x_i) - y_i)^2$.
- **Classification — accuracy / 0–1 loss:**
  $\text{accuracy} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_i \mathbf{1}\{\hat{y}_i = y_i\}$,
  and $\text{0–1 loss} = 1 - \text{accuracy}$.
- Training = **minimizing the loss** over $\theta$.

### Train/test split and generalization
- We split data into a **training set** (used to fit $\theta$) and a held-out
  **test set** (used once to estimate real-world error).
- Built **from scratch**: shuffle the row indices with `rng`, then slice.
- We **shuffle first** so sorted data doesn't poison the split.
- **Generalization** = performance on unseen data; the test error is our honest
  estimate of it.

### Overfitting, underfitting, and the U-curve
- **Underfitting** — model too simple; high error on train *and* test.
- **Overfitting** — model too flexible; tiny train error but large test error.
- Fitting polynomials of growing degree (`np.polyfit` / `np.polyval`) and
  plotting train vs test error gives the classic **U-shaped** test-error curve;
  its bottom is the best complexity.

### Bias–variance trade-off
$$\text{test error} = \text{bias}^2 + \text{variance} + \text{noise}.$$
- **Bias**: error from being too rigid (underfitting).
- **Variance**: sensitivity to the particular training sample (overfitting).
- **Noise**: irreducible randomness. The best model **balances** bias and
  variance.

## Key things that surprise newcomers

| Thing | Note |
|-------|------|
| Perfect training score | meaningless on its own — could be pure memorization |
| Why test on held-out data | the only honest estimate of generalization |
| Shuffle before splitting | sorted data can put a whole class in one set |
| More complex ≠ better | past the U's bottom, test error *rises* |
| `X` shape | $(n, d)$ = (samples, features); a transposed `X` breaks everything |

## Building the notebook

This notebook is generated from a Python script (never hand-edited JSON):

```
python tools/generators/ch09_intro_ml.py
```

## After this chapter

You know the vocabulary and the workflow of machine learning. Next we put it to
work: fitting real models with gradient descent and turning these concepts into
working learning algorithms.

## 📚 Further reading

- Andrew Ng — [Machine Learning Specialization](https://www.coursera.org/specializations/machine-learning-introduction) (Coursera, DeepLearning.AI) — the gentlest tour of these foundations.
- Stanford [CS229 lecture notes](https://cs229.stanford.edu/) — the same ideas with more math.
- [Mathematics for Machine Learning](https://mml-book.com) — Deisenroth, Faisal & Ong (free PDF) — the math underpinning every model.
